coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemical sensor based on GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs modified glassy carbon electrode for simultaneous determination of Irinotecan and 5-Fluorouracil in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Es'haghi, Zarrin
Modarres Zahed, Fatemeh
Darroudi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots-polyaniline/zinc oxide nanocomposites (GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs) was successfully synthesized and employed as excellent modifier to construct a novel and highly selective electrochemical sensing platform for the simultaneous determination of two important anticancer drugs, Irinotecan (CPT-11), and 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU). FTIR, XRD, TEM, and FE-SEM were used to characterize the synthesized NCs. A detailed study by electrochemical methods such as CV, EIS, and DPV were performed to clarify the electrocatalytic properties of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs and preparation process of the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The experimental conditions for CPT-11 and 5-FU detection were optimized by Taguchi L25 orthogonal array design, and under the optimal condition, the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE presented a linear response in the ranges 0.1–25.0 and 0.1–50.0 μM and low detection limits of 0.011 and 0.023 μM, for CPT-11 and 5-FU, respectively. The reliability of the proposed sensor was verified by precise and sensitive simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU in the pharmaceutical formulation and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Polyaniline/zinc oxide nanocomposite
Irinotecan
5-Fluorouracil
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs, PANI, ZnO-NPs, and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs
Electrochemical behavior of CPT-11 and 5-FU at GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE
Method validation and stability of the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of ZnO-NPs, PANI and PANI/ZnO-NCs, (c) TEM image and (d) size distribution of GQDs, (e) FE-SEM image and (f) size distribution of ZnO-NPs, and (g, h) FE-SEM images of PANI and PANI/ZnO-NCs.

(a) CV and (b) EIS of different modified electrodes in solution of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. (c) CV and (d) DP voltammograms of different modified electrodes in the presence of 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 20.0 μM 5-FU in BR (pH 10.0).

(a) DPVs of different concentrations of CPT-11 (0.05, 0.5, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, and 30.0 μM) in the presence of 20.0 μ M 5-FU and (b) different concentrations of 5-FU (0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, and 60.0 μM) in the presence of 10.0 μM CPT-11 at GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE, in BR (pH 10.0). Inset: the peak current versus concentration of the CPT-11 and 5-FU; (c) DPVs for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 (0.10, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, and 25.0 μM) and 5-FU (0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, and 50.0 μM) by using GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE, in BR (pH 10.0); (d) Calibration graphs for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU.
The comparison of some characteristics of CPT-11 and 5-FU at GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE with other modified electrodes in the literature.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Poly(methylene blue)/multi-walled carbon nanotube modified glassy carbon electrode.

% Relative error in the analytical signal

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The current in the absence of any interference for CPT-11 was 7.5 μA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The current in the absence of any interference for 5-FU was 7.1 μA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for DPV measurements in aqueous, pharmaceutical, serum, and urine samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % Recovery=(amount of analyte determined/amount of analyte taken)×100.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD (%) for six sets of LOD data.

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemical sensor based on GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs modified glassy carbon electrode for simultaneous determination of Irinotecan and 5-Fluorouracil in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NanoBioEletrochemistry Research Center, Bam University of Medical Sciences, Bam, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Neurogenic Inﬂammation Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, 9177948564, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nuclear Medicine Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Modern Sciences and Technologies, School of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Modern Sciences and Technologies

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nuclear Medicine Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots-polyaniline/zinc oxide nanocomposites (GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs) was successfully synthesized and employed as excellent modifier to construct a novel and highly selective electrochemical sensing platform for the simultaneous determination of two important anticancer drugs, Irinotecan (CPT-11), and 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU). FTIR, XRD, TEM, and FE-SEM were used to characterize the synthesized NCs. A detailed study by electrochemical methods such as CV, EIS, and DPV were performed to clarify the electrocatalytic properties of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs and preparation process of the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The experimental conditions for CPT-11 and 5-FU detection were optimized by Taguchi L25 orthogonal array design, and under the optimal condition, the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE presented a linear response in the ranges 0.1–25.0 and 0.1–50.0 μM and low detection limits of 0.011 and 0.023 μM, for CPT-11 and 5-FU, respectively. The reliability of the proposed sensor was verified by precise and sensitive simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU in the pharmaceutical formulation and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Irinotecan (CPT-11) as an antineoplastic drug is converted in vivo to its bioactive metabolite, 7-ethyl-10-hydroxy camptothecin (SN-38). The cytotoxic effect of the SN-38 can inhibit the activity of topoisomerase I, which plays a serious role in transcription and replication of DNA [1]. It was proved that CPT-11 has a growing clinical impact in treating ovaries, cervical, pancreas, colon, and lung cancer [2]. 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) is another anticancer drug that has been widely used to treat cervical, colon, breast, stomach, and pancreas cancer [3,4]. The antitumor activity of 5-FU is attributed to its inhibitory effect on thymidylate synthase enzyme, required for normal function of RNA and DNA [5]. Now the incorporation regimens of CPT-11 and 5-FU are commonly used as effective treatment of colon cancer [6]. The large scale therapeutic use of CPT-11 and 5-FU with maximum therapeutic effects and minimum toxicities, created the need for the development of an accurate, sensitive, and fast technique for detection of CPT-11 and 5-FU in biological samples. Thus, simultaneous analysis of CPT-11 and 5-FU is the important challenge in biological investigations. In the literature, different analytical methods, such as liquid chromatography [7–9], spectrofluorimetry [10,11], spectrophotometry [12,13], and electrochemical techniques [14,15] have been reported for the individual determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU on blood and urine samples. Our research library study revealed that no attempt has been developed so far to study the simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU by any analytical method. Compared to all the analytical methods, electrochemical techniques as a fast, reliable and inexpensive technique permits simultaneous determination of anticancer drugs in biological samples without complex pre-treatment process. Although there are some problems about the use of bare electrodes for simultaneous quantitative determination of anticancer drugs such as weak oxidation signal, high oxidation overpotential, and overlap the responses signal of drugs, but possibility of modifying the surface of electrochemical-based sensors and biosensors with various mediators particularly nano-structure mediators, has solved all these problems and made them as powerful tools for simultaneous analysis of anticancer drugs [16]. Modified working electrodes based on new conductive materials significantly improve the sensitivity and intensity of the response signal and create a superior capability for simultaneous determination of electro-active compounds in biological samples. For example, in our previous study, we simultaneously determined the concentration of ifosfamide and etoposide in biological samples and pharmaceutical compounds using a pencil graphite electrode modified by gold/palladium@reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite and poly (L-Cysteine) [17]. Karimi-Maleh et al., described the development of a sensor for simultaneous detection of 6-mercaptopurine, 6-thioguanine and dasatinib by combination platinum and multi wall carbon nanotube with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluoro phosphate, in a carbon paste matrix [18]. As well as Materon et al., recently reported the simultaneous detection of doxorubicin and methotrexate by a GCE modified with Cu nanoparticles, carbon black, and Nafion [19]. The efficiency of electrochemical sensors and biosensors is strongly dependent on the properties of materials that deposited on the surface of the electrodes [20]. To increase the performance of the sensors, different modifiers like nanomaterials [21], metals and their oxides [22], ionic liquids [23], semiconductors [24], polymers and inorganic mediators [25] have been used to electrode decorate.

Zero-dimensional GQDs, as defined, are graphene fragments with features derived from graphene, have attracted significant attention in the field of electrochemical sensors. By converting graphene sheets into GQDs, the GQDs show new phenomena because of quantum confinement and edge effects [26]. GQDs with novel properties such as small sizes, fast electron transfer rate, abundant edge sites, excellent electrical conductivity, great biocompatibility, high photo-stability and low toxicity can be regarded as promising candidates for electrochemical sensing materials [27]. Therefore, GQDs based sensors can achieve a high level of performance.

Nowadays, numerous electroconductive organic polymers have been employed to increase electrocatalytic activity of sensors and biosensors [28,29], in which nanostructured conducting polyaniline (PANI) has received remarkable attention due to its low cost, environment-friendly, unique redox behavior, easy control of synthesis, tunable electronic conductivity, and ease of blending with other materials [30,31]. However pure PANI as an electrode modifier still suffer from some problems, such as long response time and poor long-term stability [32]. Hence the nanocomposite which is composed of PANI and carbon series nano-materials (carbon nanotubes, graphene, and its derivatives) [33,34] or PANI and metal oxide semiconductors [35,36] possess the excellent features of both organic–inorganic nanocomposite materials and further improve the performance of PANI-based sensors.

Among various metal nanoparticles, zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs) as a popular n-type functional semiconductor with nice biocompatibility, high electrochemical stability, large specific area, wide band gap, nontoxicity, and controllable electrical conductivity extensively used in the field of sensing [37–39].

In present study, we report the synthesis and characterization of a novel GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs, followed by electrode surface modification by simple casting procedure to fabricate a voltammetric sensor for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU without physiological interferents. Based on our research, this is the first report for the utility of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs as electrocatalyst to develop an electrochemical sensor to quantitative determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU simultaneously. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) studies showed that the synthesized nanocomposite has a high surface area and effectively supports the electroactive species and improves the catalytic activity and the electrochemical characteristics of the sensor. Investigation of electrochemical behavior of CPT-11 and 5-FU with GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE by CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques displayed two distinct oxidation peak with good potential separation and high resolution, indicating the simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU with proposed sensor in the presence of each other is possible. In addition, this platform exhibited an overall enhanced performance for the highly selective and sensitive detection of CPT-11 and 5-FU in several complex real samples (serum, urine, and injection solutions) with trace amounts.

Irinotecan, 5-Fluorouracil, d-(+)-glucose, and ethylene diamine were prepared from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other reagents, such as Zn (CH3COO)2. 2H2O, NaCl, KCl, NaOH, CH3COOH, H3BO3, H3PO4, KH2PO4, and K2HPO4 were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). All the chemicals were of high purity (analytical grade) and used without further purification except when is mentioned specifically.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were done in an analytical system, Metrohm device 797 V A Computrace (Metrohm, Switzerland). Gill AC potentiostat (ACM instruments, UK) was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) studies. The particle size and morphology characterization of nanoparticles were done by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (ALEO 912 AB) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (TESCAN MIRA3-FEG), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was done by a X-ray analytical diffractometer X-ray (Philips analytical X-ray). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was analyzed by a Fast-Scan FT-IR Spectrometer (East Norwalk, CT, USA).

The method which developed by Gu et al., was used to prepare the GQDs [40]. In this synthesis method, a mixture of glucose (1.0 g), ethylene diamine (400 μL), and HCl (37%, w/w) (200 μL) was added to 15 mL deionized water under stirring for 30 min. The reaction mixtures were conveyed into an autoclave and the hydrothermal process was performed at 200 °C for 6 h. The brown product obtained from this step was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) and dialyzed to remove the large tracts of GO. The GQDs solution was kept at 4 °C.

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize ZnO-NPs. In this way, a solution of 0.1 M zinc acetate was prepared in 50 mL of methanol, then 25 mL 0.2 M NaOH was added in the above solution and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was conveyed into an autoclave and heated for 6 h at 100 °C. The sediments in the autoclave were collected after cooling at ambient temperature and thoroughly rinsed with absolute ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the resulting white precipitates was dried at 60 °C.

The PANI/ZnO-NCs was prepared via the following two step procedure: At first, PANI nanofibers were synthesized by dissolving aniline (0.298 g) in aqueous solution of HCl (10 mL, 1.0 M) to produce monomer solution. Then, the oxidant solution was obtained by adding ammonium persulphate (0.186 g) in HCl aqueous solution (10 mL, 1.0 M). The chemical polymerization was started by adding the oxidation solution to the monomer solution with a volume ratio of 1:4 all at once. Then, the polymerization reaction was allowed to complete for 10 min at ambient temperature. The synthesized PANI nanofibers were filtered out, rinsed with deionized water followed by vacuum drying (50 °C, 24 h) [41]. In the second step, the ZnO-NPs (3.0 mg) were mixed with PANI (2.0 mg) in DMF (2.0 mL) and then stirred for 72 h at ambient temperature until the suspension of PANI/ZnO-NCs was obtained [42].

A GCE which the surface of it thoroughly polished, washed and dried at room temperature was used as working electrode and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs thin film deposited on its surface. The fabrication strategy of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs modified GCE is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, 5.0 μL of the synthesized GQDs were dropped on the surface of the bare GCE subsequently dried at ambient temperature. Afterwards, 10 μL of the synthesized PANI/ZnO-NCs was added on the GQDs/GCE surface. The continued treatment by removing the additional solvent at 50 °C resulted in the formation of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs thin film on the GCE surface. For comparison, GQDs/GCE, GQDs-PANI/GCE, and GQDs-ZnO/GCE, were prepared with a similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, PANI, ZnO-NPs, and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs, PANI, ZnO-NPs and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs was fully characterized by techniques such as FTIR, XRD, TEM, and FE-SEM. Fig. 1 a displays the FT-IR spectra of ZnO-NPs, PANI, and PANI/ZnO-NCs. In the spectrum of ZnO-NPs, the broad band at around 3504 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching vibration mode and the absorption band at 447 cm−1 is related to vibration of Zn–O [43]. In the spectrum of PANI, band at 3220 cm−1 corresponds to NH stretching mode of PANI. The bands at 1570 and 1480 cm−1 are attributed to the CC and CN stretching mode of the quinoid and benzenoid rings in PANI [44]. The peak at 1303 cm−1 corresponds to the CN stretching vibration [36]. Also, the peaks at 1181 and 799 cm−1 are related to the in-plane bonding of CH bond and out of plane bonding of CH bond of PANI, respectively [28]. In the spectrum of PANI/ZnO-NCs, the specific bonds of ZnO-NPs and PANI are observed, but peak intensity and peak positions indicate a slight change compared to the ZnO-NPs or PANI spectrum, demonstrating the PANI/ZnO-NCs have been successfully prepared.

The XRD patterns of ZnO-NPs, PANI, and PANI/ZnO-NCs are presented in Fig. 1b. In the XRD pattern of PANI, two peaks at 2θ = 20° and 26° can be selected for the specific (020) and (200) planes of PANI [45]. When the PANI/ZnO-NCs was formed, the PANI specific peaks at 2θ = 20° and 26° were significantly reduced, and new diffraction peaks were appeared at 2θ = 33.05°, 34.49°, 37.62°, 47.59°, 57.27°, 62.87°, and 67.87°, corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112) planes, respectively [46] as presence confirming of ZnO-NPs in the PANI/ZnO-NCs.

The typical TEM and FE-SEM were employed to characterize the surface morphology of synthesized nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1c, uniform spherical shape of GQDs was clearly observed, and the histogram of the synthesized GQDs in Fig. 1d showed that the size of GQDs is ˜ 5 nm. FE-SEM image also exhibits uniform spherical morphology of ZnO-NPs (Fig. 1e) and Fig. 1f shows that the size of ZnO-NPs is ˜ 50 nm. Fig. 1(g and h) displays the FE-SEM images of PANI and PANI/ZnO-NCs. As can be seen from Fig. 1g, the dominant morphology of PANI is a nanostructure with fibrous form which is closed because of the π-π interaction between PANI molecules. For PANI/ZnO-NCs (Fig. 1h), the distribution pattern between ZnO-NPs and the PANI revealed that the PANI nanofibers were surrounded by ZnO-NPs. It can be considered that the ionic interaction between PANI and the ZnO-NPs has led to the successful formation of PANI/ZnO-NCs [42].

Among the various types of sensors, the electrochemical sensors are the most common to investigate the bio-interaction and detection process. Practical principle of sensors consists of a transducer element covered by a recognizing coating material. The coating interacts with target analyte and signal is created. Electrochemical transducers then transform the chemical changes into electrical signals which related to the concentration of analyte.

The electrochemical performance of different modified electrodes were evaluated using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− mixture containing KCl (0.1 M) as redox probes under the same conditions, by CV and EIS techniques. As shown in Fig. 2 a, with modification of the electrode surface by GQDs, GQDs-PANI, GQDs-ZnO, and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs, the peak current intensity increases and the peak potential separation (ΔEP) decreases, which indicates the improvement of the electrochemical performance of the sensor due to the increase in catalytic ability, electron transfer rate and conductivity. However, greatest peak current and lowest potential peak separation are observed when the surface of the electrode is decorated with GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs, which can be related to the synergistic contribution between GQDs, PANI, and ZnO-NPs. In further experiments, the effective area of the electrodes were determined using Randles-Sevick formula [47] in probe solution (1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−). Based on the slope of Ip versus v1/2 plot, the active electrode area of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE was calculated 0.627 ± 0.004 cm2, which was 1.18 times that of GQDs-ZnO/GCE (0.532 ± 0.003 cm2), 1.62 times that of GQDs-PANI/GCE (0.386 ± 0.003 cm2), 1.77 times that of GQDs/GCE (0.354 ± 0.005 cm2) and 2.71 times that of bare GCE (0.231 ± 0.004 cm2).

To study the charge transfer ability of various electrodes, EIS analysis was also performed. The impedance curve (Nyquist plot) includes a semicircular region at high frequencies corresponding to the electron transfer resistance (Ret) and a linear region at low frequencies corresponding to the diffusion process [48]. In Fig. 2b it can be observed that the smaller semicircle domains obtained on GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE, proved its lower electron transfer resistance. This result clearly confirms that the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs have high conductivity, unique catalytic ability and high specific surface area that effectively increases the conductivity performance of the electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of CPT-11 and 5-FU at GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: To study the response of the fabricated GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE to CPT-11 and 5-FU, comparative voltammetric measurements by CV method were performed in binary mixture containing CPT-11 (10.0 μM) and 5-FU (20.0 μM) with Britton-Robinson buffer (BR) (pH 10.0) as supporting electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 2c, two broad and very weak oxidation peaks were appeared on the unmodified GCE indicating slow electron transfer rate on the surface of unmodified GCE. In contrast, a pair of well-defined oxidation peaks with a systematic increase in the peak currents for CPT-11 and 5-FU was recorded on GQDs/GCE, GQDs-PANI/GCE, and GQDs-ZnO/GCE, indicating great activity of modifying materials for the electrochemical oxidation of CPT-11 and 5-FU. However, GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE displayed a pair of well-defined redox peaks with higher current values in comparison to bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, GQDs-PANI/GCE, and GQDs-ZnO/GCE. This demonstrate that combination of GQDs, PANI, and ZnO nanoparticles can mostly facilitate the electron transfer between CPT-11 and 5-FU and electrode surface and thus improve the catalytic activity of the sensor.

The electrochemical behavior of CPT-11 and 5-FU in BR solution of pH 10.0 was also investigated by DPV method. Fig. 2d shows the DP voltammograms of 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 20.0 μM 5-FU at the surface of different electrodes. It is observed that the oxidation peaks of CPT-11 and 5-FU increase continuously with successive modification of the electrode surfaces with GQDs, GQDs-PANI, GQDs-ZnO, and GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs as well as shift of potentials peak toward the negative (-) direction. These results confirm the results of CV studies and prove that GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs is promising modifier to study the simultaneous oxidation of CPT-11 and 5-FU.

The results of these experiments showed that the most important principle of this novel and facile strategy for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU is the use of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs as a unique electrode surface modifier. The GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs provide active sites for direct interaction of target analytes (CPT-11 and 5-FU) to the electrode surface as well as facilitate the electron transfer process between the analytes and the electrode surface. As a result, two distinct peak with high-resolution and appropriate potential separation appear in the CV and DPV methods, which are used to simultaneously determine CPT-11 and 5-FU.

The Taguchi experimental design method was used to optimize the important and influential factors in the construction process of the sensor. In the fabrication of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE, the effect of four important factor which included the amount of GQDs, the ratio of PANI:ZnO in the synthesis of PANI/ZnO-NCs, stirring time to form the PANI/ZnO-NCs and the amount of PANI/ZnO-NCs was investigated, via Taguchi L25 orthogonal array design (Tables S1 and S2). In this study, the peak currents recorded for CPT-11 (10.0 μM) and 5-FU (20.0 μM) with three repetitions was considered as the response of the experiments. To further validate the experimental design, ANOVA analysis was also carried out [49]. Based on the details provided in the supplementary materials (Tables S3 and S4 and also Fig. S1) the ideal conditions for fabrication of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE; 5.0 μL of GQDs, 2:3 ratio of PANI:ZnO in the synthesis of PANI/ZnO-NCs, 72 h to form the PANI/ZnO-NCs and 10 μL of PANI/ZnO-NCs.

To study the influence of the pH on oxidation process of CPT-11 and 5-FU at GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE, different BR buffer solutions in the pH range of 5.0 to 11.0 were prepared and DP voltammograms of 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 20.0 μM 5-FU were recorded in these solutions as electrolyte solution. It can be seen that by increasing pH over the range 5.0–10.0, the peak currents of CPT-11 and 5-FU slightly increase and then they decrease at higher pH (Fig. S2a). However, the results show that the CPT-11 and 5-FU have obtained the maximum peak current and adequate peak separation in pH 10.0. Hence, BR buffer with pH 10.0 was selected as the optimum pH for simultaneously determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU. It is also observed that, following the pH from 5.0 to 10.0, the potentials of oxidation peaks of CPT-11 and 5-FU moved negatively, indicating the participation of protons in the electrode reactions. The linear relationship between peak potentials of CPT-11 and 5-FU and pH is shown in Figs. S2b and c and obeys the following equations:
(1) Epa (CPT-11) = -0.0550 pH + 1.2857; R2 = 0.9973
(2) Epa (5-FU) = -0.0596 pH + 1.6343; R2 = 0.9987

According to Nernst equation [50]:
(3)
E
p a
= -
0.0592
m
n
p H
+ b
Where n and m represent the electron transfer number and the number of protons participating in the oxidation reaction, respectively. The m/n was calculated as 0.93 for CPT-11 and 1.01 for 5-FU. Based to the obtained results, the electrons and protons were identical during the electrode reaction.

To determine the number of electrons participating in the electrochemical reactions, the influence of scan rate on 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 20.0 μM 5-FU oxidation at the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE was studied via CV technique through varying the scan rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1. The results presented in Figs. S3 and S4 revealed a linear increase of anodic peak currents (Ip) of CPT-11 and 5-FU with square root of the scan rates (v1/2) as well as the shift of peak potentials to more positive values. Besides, an excellent linear dependence between the log Ip and the log v with slope values of 0.5259 for CPT-11 and 0.4797 for 5-FU were obtained, which further verified the electrode reaction process of CPT-11 and 5-FU on GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE was controlled by diffusion [51]. As well a linear dependence between the oxidation peak potential of CPT-11 and 5-FU and the logarithm of scan rate were exposed as:
(4) Epa (CPT-11) (V) = 0.0907 log v (V s−1) + 0.9068, R2 = 0.9945
(5) Epa (5-FU) (V) = 0.0844 log v (V s−1) + 1.1980, R2 = 0.9945

By means of Laviron model the electron transfer number in electrochemical reaction can be calculated [52]:
(6)
E
p a
=
E 0
+ R T /
( 1 - α ) n F
log v
Where, Eº, α, and n refers to the formal potential, charge transfer coefficient and electron transfer number in electrochemical oxidation. Other factors, such as F, T, and R, are in their normal meaning. Considering the α = 0.5 for irreversible reactions and based on the slope of Eqs. (4) and (5), the electron transfer number in electrochemical oxidation of CPT-11 and 5-FU were calculated to be 1.3 and 1.4, respectively (approximately equal to 1), which was consistent with previous studies [53–55].

Under the conditions of optimal, the sensor performance was evaluated for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU by DPV in BR buffer (pH 10.0). For this, DPVs of each analyte in the mixture was recorded over when the concentration of one drug changed and another drug kept unchanged. As observed in Fig. 3 a, the peak current value of CPT-11 increased linearly with continuous addition of CPT-11 from 0.05 to 30.0 μM in the presence of 20.0 μM 5-FU. Favorable linearity for CPT-11 concentration was expressed as Ip (μA) = (0.4218 ± 0.0162) CCPT-11 (μM) + (2.5473 ± 0.1025) (R2 = 0.9925). Likewise, in Fig. 3b, it was observed that the peak current value of 5-FU increased linearly with successive addition of 5-FU from 0.1 to 60.0 μM in the presence of 10.0 μM CPT-11. Favorable linearity for 5-FU concentration was expressed as: Ip (μA) = (0.3860 ± 0.0184) CCPT-11 (μM) + (2.6260 ± 0.1141) (R2 = 0.9950). These results clearly showed that the change in the concentration of one drug had no remarkable influence on the electrochemical signal of another drug. Finally, to further investigate the applicability of the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE for the simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU, the analytical curves were obtained by varying the concentrations of both drugs simultaneously from 0.1 to 25.0 μM for CPT-11 and 0.1 to 50.0 μM for 5-FU (Fig. 3c and d). The linear regression equations are exposed as follow:
(7) Ip (μA) = (0.6333 ± 0.0137) CCPT-11 (μM) + (1.2582 ± 0.1104) (R2 = 0.9986)
(8) Ip (μA) = (0.3724 ± 0.0169) C5-FU (μM) + (2.9325 ± 0.1542) (R2 = 0.9924)

The detection limits (based on 3Sb/m) (Sb is the standard deviation of the blank in six runs and m is the slope of the calibration curve) [56] were calculated to be 0.011 μM and 0.023 μM for CPT-11 and 5-FU, respectively. It should be noted that no reports are available on the simultaneous electrochemical determination of these drugs. It is imperative to compare the advantages of the present study with the other sensors reported for CPT-11 or 5-FU determination in literature. As can be seen in Table 1 , the linear response range for CPT-11 and 5-FU was wider than PGE [14,57], SMDE (tacc = 20 s) [58], and GNP-MWCNT- Chit/GCE [60], as well as the detection limit was lower than PMB/MWCNT/ GCE [53], SMDE (tacc = 20 s) [58], CPE/AuNps-PFR [59], and ZnFe2O4/MNPs/IL/CPE [55]. Although few modified electrodes showed advantageous linear range or detection limit, it was also evident from Table 1 that the performance of GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE is quite comparable or even better than the previously reported electrochemical sensors, revealing the ability of the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE towards sensitive determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation and stability of the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of the fabricated GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE towards simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU under optimized conditions was investigated by DPV measurements of 10.0 and 20.0 μM CPT-11 and 5-FU, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for six successive assays by the same electrode was 3.26% and 3.38%, respectively, demonstrating satisfactory repeatability of the sensor. To evaluate the reproducibility of this modified working electrode, six consecutive measurements were done based on DPV responses of CPT-11 and 5-FU by six different electrodes which were made under same processing conditions. The value of RSD% was 3.62% for 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 3.75% for 20.0 μM 5-FU in 6 intra-day experiments while they were obtained 4.13% for 10.0 μM CPT-11 and 4.20% for 20.0 μM 5-FU in the 6 inter-day experiments. Furthermore the peak currents measured every three days on the same GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE showed that the electrode responses toward CPT-11 and 5-FU were decreased less than 4.5% compared with the original responses after 30 days, demonstrating the fabricated sensor possessed excellent long-term stability.

The specific recognition ability of GCE modified with GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs was evaluated by measuring DPV responses of 10 μM CPT-11 and 5-FU in the presence of some ions, several drugs that can be given at the same time as CPT-11 or 5-FU and various potentially electroactive biological compounds. According to the obtained results, existence of different interfering molecules had a negligible effect on DPV responses, consequently the oxidation peak currents of CPT-11 and 5-FU were influenced less than 5% in the presence of 30 μM of oxaliplatin, ifosfamide, etoposide, and flutamide, 50 μM of dopamine, folic acid and caffeine, 4000 μM of
K +
,
Na +
,
M g
2 +
,
C a
2 +
,
C O
3
2 -
, and
N O
3 -
, as well as 2000 μM of ascorbic acid, citric acid, uric acid, glucose, dextrose, lactose (Table 2 ). These experimental results, clearly proved that GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE has great anti-interference capability for selective detection of CPT-11 and 5-FU.

Standard addition method was used to directly determine the CPT-11 and 5-FU concentrations in the injection solution forms of CPT-11 (Ieinotecan hydrochloride 100 mg/5 mL injection) and 5-FU (Fluracil 250 mg/5 mL injection), blood serum and urine samples. A statistical analysis of the obtained calibration curves are summarized in Table 3 . The wide linear determination range between 0.1–25.0 μM for CPT-11 and 0.1–50.0 for 5-FU in the matrix of all real samples (pharmaceutical samples, blood serum and urine), excellent recoveries as well as great detection limits indicating that GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE as a working electrode presents precise and accurate results in real sample matrixes.

This paper describes electrocatalytic oxidation of CPT-11 and 5-FU on a rapid, selective, sensitive, and stable sensor based on combination of GQDs and PANI/ZnO-NCs. The fabricated sensor showed excellent electrochemical catalytic activities towards CPT-11 and 5-FU, because of the synergistic effects of GQDs, PANI and ZnO nanostructures. Therefore, the GQDs-PANI/ZnO-NCs/GCE was successfully applied for the electrochemical sensing of a simultaneous mixture of CPT-11 and 5-FU with excellent sensitivity, desirable linear ranges and low detection limits in real samples. When compared with the other existing methods for the simultaneous determination of drugs (often chromatography methods), the developed method can be successfully used to determine the CPT-11 and 5-FU simultaneously, because of reported methods suffer from various drawbacks e.g., time-consuming, high cost, multiple steps, and complex protocol. However, this sensor for the first time offered a facile technique for simultaneous determination of CPT-11 and 5-FU with a short analysis time coupled with excellent anti-interference ability, repeatability, reproducibility, long-term stability and cost-effectiveness. The present study could provide a favorable perspective in the fabrication of electrochemical platforms based on electrodes modified with various nanostructures to simultaneously determine at least two anticancer drugs. It should be noted that for the successful construction of the electrode, especially when the number of experimental parameters is numerous, the use of experimental design methods is highly recommended.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences for the technical and financial support (Grant no. 970959) of this work.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Benaz Hatamluyi received her PhD from Payame Noor University in 2018. Her research interests are mainly focusing on synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in fields of electrochemical sensors and biosensors.

Zarrin Es’haghi is PhD in Anal. Chem. She obtained her degree in Analytical Chemistry in the Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran, in 2004. She is currently a Professor of Analytical chemistry at Payame Noor University, Mashhad center, Iran. She has worked in different research lines in the frame of nanotechnology from an analytical point of view, such as the identification of compounds present in food, drug etc. based on microextraction methods, the development of analytical methodologies for its quantification and the development of new nanoadsorbent materials. She also collaborates in the teaching of some subjects of the analytical chemistry area. Her scientific career can be reflected in 14 participations in research projects, 109 scientific publications and more than 70 presentations in national and international congresses. She has also supervised 9 doctoral theses, more than seventy master's theses and several final degree projects.
Fatemeh Modarres Zahed received her Master degree of Analytical Chemistry from Payame Noor University in 2018. Her research interests focus on electrochemical sensors.

Majid Darroudi, assistant professor of Nanoscience at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences with research interests in green synthesis of different nanomaterials and investigation of their applications in medicine and pharmacy, published 100+ peer-reviewed international papers with 3 chapter books in both fields.
